coredata.dc:title: Thermal runaway characteristics of a LiFePO4-based lithium-ion secondary battery using the laser-irradiation method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shironita, Sayoko
Tsuruga, Hideki
Honda, Keizoh
Koshika, Kenichiroh
Umeda, Minoru
coredata.dc:description: The establishment of a test method is a prerequisite to ensure the safety of lithium-ion secondary batteries. In context, laser irradiation is a heating technique that causes explosion of the batteries and is deemed as a safety test according to international standards. In this study, we elucidate the characteristics of laser irradiation heating by subjecting a 18650-type battery with LiFePO4 as the cathode material to laser irradiation to cause thermal runaway. Subsequently, we estimated the thermal runaway process and compared the heating method with those of an accelerating rate calorimeter furnace and a ceramic heater. The experimental results revealed that the thermal runaway was activated at the laser-irradiated small area by immediate heating. Initially, the separator shrink occurs, which was followed by an internal short circuit caused by the subsequent heating, and further heating resulted in thermal runaway. The findings of this study indicate that an extremely high power per unit area is generated by laser irradiation heating, and the battery heat generation rate during thermal runaway is equivalent to that obtained by a ceramic heater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Laser irradiation
Thermal runaway
Accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC)
Battery safety
LFP-based lithium-ion secondary battery
18650-type

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Observation of battery appearance and inside
Thermal properties of the separator and battery
Thermal properties of the separator sheet
Thermal property of battery measured by ARC
Laser irradiation to battery by varying input energy
Observation of laser-irradiated batteries by varying input energy
Thermal phenomena of laser irradiated batteries by varying input energy
Thermal phenomena of batteries heated by laser, ceramic heater, and ARC furnace
Advantages of the laser irradiation procedure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been partially supported by the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT), Japan. The authors acknowledge Dr. T. Honma and Ms. Y. Du for their assistance with the TMA and DSC measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) DSC measurement of separator sample; arrows (i) and (ii) indicate endothermic reactions at 125 °C and 155 °C, respectively. TMA measurements using the separator: (b) length of sample vs. temperature, and (c) dL/dT vs. temperature. Separator sample was removed at 0% SOC from battery for safety.

Photographs of separator samples: (a) before and after heating at (b) 120, (c) 130, (d) 150, (e) 160, and (f) 170 °C in air atmosphere; both heating and cooling rates were 5 °C min–1. Separator sample was removed at 0% SOC from battery for safety.

(a) Surface temperature profile of the battery during ARC test. Variations in (b) heat generation rate dT/dt, and (c) OCV and resistance with respect to battery surface temperature during ARC heating (100% SOC). Point A indicated a rapid change in internal resistance (i.e., separator shutdown), point B corresponds to vent release [24, 27, 28], and point C indicates the occurrence of short circuit.

Temporal variation of battery surface temperature and OCV for various energies and time periods of laser irradiation, such as (a) 0.08 kJ (0.4 s), (b) 4.6 kJ (80 s), and (c) 6.9 kJ (120 s) (100% SOC).

(a) X-ray CT image, and (b) SEM image of cross-section of battery after laser irradiation of 0.08 kJ (100% SOC). Batteries of (a) and (b) are with and without outer can, respectively. (c) Schematic illustration of (b).
SEM image of battery cross-section after laser irradiation of 4.6 kJ on battery with 0% SOC.
Temporal variation in battery surface temperature and OCV during heating by ceramic heater (100% SOC). Heater was stopped after 10.7 min.

(a) Heat generation rate (dT/dt), and (b) variations in OCV as functions of battery surface temperature during laser irradiation, heating by a ceramic heater, and heating in an ARC furnace. Arrows and broken lines in (a) indicate the stopping of laser irradiation/heating and the decreased temperature of the separator from Fig. 1(c), respectively; arrows in (b) indicate points at which the OCV reduces to 0 V.

Comparison of laser irradiation energies in sections heated by laser irradiation (orange markers) and region of separator loss (blue marks) at SOC 0 and 100%. Maximum temperature measured from Figs. 4(a)–(c) and Fig. 6.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental conditions of thermal runaway occurring in batteries in response to three heating methods (laser irradiation, ceramic heater, and ARC furnace). Red marks indicate the irradiated/heated area.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Accumulated energy before/after adiabatic mode using the average power of the ARC furnace.

head.ce:title: Thermal runaway characteristics of a LiFePO4-based lithium-ion secondary battery using the laser-irradiation method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Technology, Nagaoka University of Technology, 1603–1, Kamitomioka, Nagaoka, Niigata 940-2188, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kansai Laboratory, Japan Electrical Safety & Environment Technology Laboratories, 4–1, Koyo-cho Nishi, Higashinada-ku, Kobe, Hyogo 658-0033, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environment Research Department, National Traffic Safety and Environment Laboratory, 7-42-27, Jindaiji-higashimachi, Chofu, Tokyo 182-0012, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Traffic Safety and Environment Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cEnvironment Research Department, National Traffic Safety and Environment Laboratory, 7-42-27, Jindaiji-higashimachi, Chofu, Tokyo 182-0012, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The establishment of a test method is a prerequisite to ensure the safety of lithium-ion secondary batteries. In context, laser irradiation is a heating technique that causes explosion of the batteries and is deemed as a safety test according to international standards. In this study, we elucidate the characteristics of laser irradiation heating by subjecting a 18650-type battery with LiFePO4 as the cathode material to laser irradiation to cause thermal runaway. Subsequently, we estimated the thermal runaway process and compared the heating method with those of an accelerating rate calorimeter furnace and a ceramic heater. The experimental results revealed that the thermal runaway was activated at the laser-irradiated small area by immediate heating. Initially, the separator shrink occurs, which was followed by an internal short circuit caused by the subsequent heating, and further heating resulted in thermal runaway. The findings of this study indicate that an extremely high power per unit area is generated by laser irradiation heating, and the battery heat generation rate during thermal runaway is equivalent to that obtained by a ceramic heater.

ce:section.ce:para: Thermal propagation tests assess the safety of lithium-ion secondary batteries [1-7]. The initiation method of thermal propagation tests can be classified into heating, overcharge, and nail penetration of the battery. In context, laser irradiation has been described as a heating method in IEC62619(2017) of the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) [8]. Although laser methods are specified in the IEC—an international standard test method that is expected to have widespread use, there are no academic articles on laser irradiation used in thermal runaway tests of batteries, except for studies on laser impact [9]. Therefore, this study can contribute toward the implementation of thermal propagation tests by elucidating the following: (i) the thermal runaway process of batteries resulting from laser irradiation and (ii) laser irradiation characteristics when compared to alternative heating methods, including deformation of the battery materials and self-heating behavior.

Prior research on laser-irradiation applications include material processing such as welding, joining, and cutting. In addition, laser absorption and reflection on materials, including the formation process of melt pools and keyholes occurring at high-temperature heating, are the key topics of material processing studies [10–13]. The materials used in laser processing are predominantly bulk materials such as metals and alloys, which do not generate self-heating similar to batteries. Thus, existing research methods are primarily focused on the observation of cross sections and deformation, rather than performing thermal property assessments of materials.

Moreover, the heating behavior of a single battery [14-20] as well as the influence of thermal properties of battery constituent materials [21] on the safety of batteries involve the investigation of thermal runaway. For instance, the self-heating of batteries, including the self-heating onset temperature and heat generation rate is investigated using an accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC). In addition, differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) is an alternative representative method for evaluating the thermal properties of battery constituent materials based on the assessments of their features such as endo-/exothermic reaction, thermal decomposition reactions, and thermal deformation. Consequently, several researchers have combined these two assessment methods, namely, ARC and DSC [2, 22, 23], to discuss the thermal runaway mechanism based on the thermalstability of battery materials such as electrode, electrolyte, and separator.

This study aims to investigate the thermal runaway process occurring in a single battery via laser irradiation for clarifying the characteristics of the laser heating performance. A 18650-type battery containing LiFePO4 (LFP) as a cathode active material was used in this study, which exhibits a relatively high thermal stability and rarely cause thermal runaway. Accordingly, the laser irradiation conditions with and without thermal runaway were observed on the 18650-type LFP battery. In addition, a self-heating phenomenon and reduction in the open circuit voltage (OCV) resulting from the laser irradiation were recorded and subsequently compared with the ARC test results. Moreover, the thermal runaway process was estimated based on this comparison, which included the thermal property of the separator as well. Furthermore, the estimated thermal runaway process was verified based on results of a partial heating test using a ceramic heater. Finally, the practical use of laser irradiation is discussed as the initiation method of the thermal propagation test.

The specifications of the 18650-type battery (O'CELL NEW Energy Technology Co., Ltd) used in this study are listed in Table 1 , wherein the LFP and graphite were used as cathode and anode active materials, respectively. In this study, we used a relatively thermally stable LFP-based battery to investigate the process resulting in thermal runaway, which can be defined as a phenomenon that occurs when the heat generation rate exceeds 1 °C min–1. Prior to the test, pre-discharging and two cycles of charge/discharge were performed under the following conditions to stabilize the battery capacity. The pre-discharging was conducted in a constant current (CC) mode with a lower voltage limit of 2.0 V and a discharge rate of 0.2 C (300 mA). Thereafter, the charging was performed in the constant current/constant voltage (CC/CV) mode with an upper voltage limit of 3.65 V, a CV cutoff of 0.05 C (75 mA), and a charging rate of 0.2 C (300 mA). Subsequently, the discharge was conducted in the CC mode with a lower voltage limit of 2.0 V and a discharge rate of 0.2 C (300 mA). The discharge capacities of the second cycle on all used cells were confirmed as larger than the nominal capacity. Furthermore, the battery was charged to a state of charge (SOC) of 100% using the above-mentioned charging method before conducting the test procedures. This schematic of this charge/dischage procedure is illustrated in Fig. A.1. Unless otherwise stated, all the batteries used in the tests had a 100% SOC because LFP batteries have a relatively high thermal stability and rarely cause thermal runaway.

A continuous-wave (CW) disk laser with a maximum output of 2000 W was employed to irradiate the battery. The laser power was set to 200 W with repeated irradiation of 0.4 s on the battery at an interval of 1.0 s, which is equivalent to an average laser power of 57.1 W. These irradiation and power conditions were considered as the input of laser energy to the battery as heat based on preliminary experiments. The irradiation periods were 0.4, 80, and 120 s, and their corresponding energies were 0.08 kJ (200 W × 0.4 s), 4.6 kJ (57.1 W × 80 s), and 6.9 kJ (57.1 W × 120 s), respectively. The laser irradiation to the cylindrical battery was controlled on the side surface at 30 mm from the anode end, as depicted in Figs. A.2 and A.3(a), where two thermocouples were placed on the battery surface 10 and 50 mm from the anode and cathode end, respectively.

The thermal property of the battery was examined using an accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC, model ES, thermal hazard technology, UK). The heat–wait–seek (HWS) mode was employed. Initially, the furnace temperature was raised at a rate of 5 °C min–1 using the heat mode. In the following 30 min, the heat generation rate of the battery was measured using the wait and seek mode. The heat mode was activated for heat generation rates below 0.05 °C min–1 [24, 25]. As the heat generation rate exceeded 0.05 °C min–1, the ARC furnace shifted to the adiabatic mode, and the furnace temperature followed the battery surface temperature. According to a previous study, the thermal runaway was defined as a condition resulting under heat generation rates > 1 °C min–1 [24, 26]. In addition, a thermocouple was fixed at the center of the battery surface to record the temperature. During the ARC test, the OCV and internal resistance of the battery were measured using an electrometer (Hokuto Denko, HE–104) and a milliohmmeter (Agilent 4338B), respectively. The experimental setup of the electrometer and milliohmmeter is presented in [24, 25].

For comparison, a micro-ceramic heater with dimensions 10 × 10 × 2 mm3 (MS1010, 40 V, 40 W, SAKAGUCHI E.H VOC Corp.) was employed to heat the battery. The voltage of the heater was controlled at 40 V using a variable transformer. The heating time was 10.7 min. Moreover, a copper plate processed according to the side surface shape of the battery was inserted between the battery and ceramic heater to facilitate effective heat transfer (refer to Fig. A.4). In addition, two thermocouples were set at the same positions as stated above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of battery appearance and inside
ce:section.ce:para: A laser beam of 0.08 kJ (irradiation time of 0.4 s) was irradiated on the side surface of the battery with SOC = 100% to observe the alterations in the appearance of the battery and assess the impact of laser irradiation aprt from imparting heat. In addition, the place of the laser irradiation was accurately determined using 3-D X-ray computed tomography (CT). Subsequently, the section was machined and polished for its internal observation using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Moreover, an additional battery was exposed to a significantly higher power of 4.6 kJ (80 s) to compare the influence of irradiation power. For these high-power exposures, the appearance observation was conducted using battery with a SOC of 100%. The internal obsevation was conducted using a battery with a SOC of 0% to ensure safety and appropriately understand the variations in SOC.

An unused LFP-based battery at SOC 0% was disassembled, and the separator sheet was carefully pulled out. Thereafter, the separator was thoroughly washed with water, punched with a 5-mm-diameter hole, and placed into an aluminum crimp pan for the DSC (DSC-60, Shimadzu) measurement. The temperature was raised from 30 to 250 °C with a heating rate of 2 °C min–1. All the measurements were recorded in an air atmosphere.

In addition, the separator pieces were heated upto 120, 130, 150, 160, and 170 °C using an aluminum crimp pan with a heating rate of 5 °C min–1 in the DSC equipment. As the final temperature was realized, the pan was cooled to 30 °C with a rate of 5 °C min–1. Thereafter, the sample was removed from the DSC equipment for observations on their morphological variations using an optical microscope.

Further, deformation of the 35-μm-thick separator sheet was measured using a thermomechanical analyzer (TMA, Thermo plus EVO2, Rigaku) under a stress of –250 mN in the temperature range from 25.7 to 300 °C with a rate of 5 °C min–1 in the air atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal properties of the separator and battery
ce:section.ce:para: A primary objective of this study is to understand the thermal properties of the separator sheet incorporated in the battery. Consequently, the heat generation property of the 18650-type battery was investigated using an ARC, which recorded the variations in OCV and resistance magnitude under conditions of uniform heating of the battery. The deformation and heat generation properties of the interior components of the battery were estimated by combining the results of the thermal property of the separator sheet, OCV, and resistance magnitude of the 18650-type battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal properties of the separator sheet
ce:section.ce:para: The results the DSC measurement on the separator sample are presented in Fig. 1 (a), wherein two endothermic reactions occurring at around 125 °C (the first reaction indicated by (i) in the figure) and 155 °C (the second reaction indicated by (ii) in the figure) could be observed. Based on the DSC results, the optical microscope photographs were captured after the separator sample was initially heated to respective temperatures of 120, 130, 150, 160, and 170 °C, and subsequently, cooled to room temperature at 5 °C min–1, as portrayed in Fig. 2 . Upon comparing Figs. 2(a) and 2(b), the sample before heating was observed to be almost circular in shape, whereas the sample heated to 120 °C exhibited a slightly modified elliptical shape. Upon comparing Fig. 2(b) and (c) with Fig. 2(d), the sample color varied from white to transparent depending on the final temperature after heating. As can be observed from Figs. 2(d), (e), and (f), the size of the sample decreased from all dimensions, and the sample continued to deform with the increasing temperature. Based on these observations, the first and second endothermic reactions depicted in Fig. 1(a) were considered to be a pore-closing reaction and thermal-shrinkage deformation, respectively. The first color change of the separator in Fig. 2 could be caused by the shutdown of the ionic conduction between the anode and cathode. Nevertheless, the small shrinkage in the separator size (see Fig. 2(b)) may have generated a small internal short circuit. Moreover, the second size modification potentially caused a direct contact between the anode and cathode.

The TMA results are presented in Fig. 1(b) and (c), which compare the temperature with the length of the sample and dL/dt, respectively. These results signified the second endothermic reaction of the DSC. As the separator in the battery was under tension, the thermal property derived from the TMA results appropriately revealed the condition inside the battery. These TMA results indicated that the primary thermal deformations occurred at around 155–180 and 195–220 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal property of battery measured by ARC
ce:section.ce:para: The variation of the battery surface temperature with the measurement time under uniform heating using the HWS mode of the ARC is presented in Fig. 3 (a). The heat generation rates (dT/dt) were derived from the results and plotted against the surface temperature (Fig. 3(b)), which exhibited repeatability. In Fig. 3(a) and (b), the heat generation rate exceeded 0.05 °C min–1, which signified the self-heating condition. Subsequently, the battery subsequently switched to the adiabatic mode beyond 175 °C. Fig. 3(c) illustrates the variations in the OCV and internal resistance magnitude with the surface temperature of the battery. A rapid increase in the internal resistance by almost two orders of magnitude and a reduction in the OCV by a few mV were observed at point A (approximately 125 °C). Based the first endothermic reaction of DSC in Fig. 1(a), this rapid change in the internal resistance was potentialy caused by a separator shutdown, which completely closed the pore. Moreover, based on the analysis of Fig. 2(c), a slightly deformed size of the separator was considered to cause a partial internal short circuit.

Furthermore, the OCV varied and the resistance decreased drastically at the point B in Fig. 3(c), where the surface temperature was found to decrease as well (Fig. 3(a)). This temperature reduction was caused by the internal pressure variation from the vent release [24, 27, 28]. The second endothermic reaction temperature of the separator in Fig. 1(a), the results portrayed in Fig. 2(d), (e), and (f), and the first thermal deformation temperature of the TMA (Fig. 1(c)) confirmed that the shrinkage of the separator induced an internal short circuit, which was further considered to trigger Joule heat, thereby resulting in the self-heating observed at 175 °C. The point C in Fig. 3(c) indicated the occurrence of a complete short-circuit, wherein further heating with a rate above 0.5 °C min–1 could be observed from Fig. 3(b). This temperature region around point C corresponded to the second deformation temperature of the TMA. In Fig. 1(b), the initial length of the separator sample varied at temperatures above 200 °C, which was the onset temperature of thermal runaway (heat generation rate > 1 °C min–1) in Fig. 3(a).

One of the two objectives of this section is to elucidate the variations in temperature at the measurement point and in the OCV of the battery using laser irradiation. The second objective is to observe morphological changes in the laser irradiated parts of the battery materials inside/outside the battery. In this study, “soft short circuits” are defined as those where the OCV drops by several dozens of mV, and “hard short circuits” are defined as cases when the OCV drops to 0 V [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Laser irradiation to battery by varying input energy
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a), (b), and (c) depict the temporal dependence of the battery surface temperature with varying laser energy, including variations in OCV. These results exhibited repeatability. In Fig. 4(a), for a laser irradiation energy of 0.08 kJ (irradiation time of 0.4 s), the OCV dropped from 3.35 V to 3.29 V after a single shot of irradiation. Although the OCV gradually recovered to 3.32 V after approximately 50 s of irradiation, it was still 30 mV lesser than that before the laser irradiation, which suggested that this phenomenon represented a soft short circuit [29]. The battery surface temperature reached 37 °C and decreased thereafter. Furthermore, temperature measurements were continued for 1 h, although no evidence of thermal runaway was observed. This temperature increase was explained by considering that the soft short circuit partially caused the Joule heating and raised the battery temperature. After the experiment, a burnt mark was observed on the exterior of the battery (Fig. A.3(b)).

The results presented in Fig. 4(b) were obtained using laser irradiation with an energy of 4.6 kJ. The OCV rapidly decreased to 0 V with intermediate up and down peaks. After the confirmation of OCV = 0 V, the laser irradiation ceased after 80 s. As the OCV did not recover after the irradiation ceased, the situation was considered as a hard short circuit. Although the surface temperature of the battery was approximately 150 °C at the instant the irradiation ceased, it gradually increased up to approximately 220 °C after 120 s of stopping the irradiation (after a total of 200 s since the onset of the irradiation). Thereafter, the temperature gradually decreased, and no thermal runaway was observed.

Fig. 4(c) illustrates the result for the additional battery sample subjected to a laser irradiation of 6.9 kJ energy (irradiation time of 120 s). In this case, the OCV reduced to almost 0 V in the range of 30–40 s. Although the OCV recovered to 2.0 V at an instance, it ultimately dropped to almost 0 V at approximately 110 s. The thermal runaway was observed at around 120 s, following which the laser irradiation was ceased.

We compared the results of Fig. 4(b) and (c), i.e., both cases of hard short circuits, because the OCV dropped to 0 V and failed to recover. In both the cases, the OCV dropped drastically, and the surface temperature of the battery simultaneously and rapidly increased to 120 °C. This strongly suggested that the hard short circuit caused the Joule heating. Upon comparing these hard short circuits to the ARC results in Fig. 3(c), the OCV variations in both the cases agreed well, which can be explained by the self-heating observed in the ARC.

Fig. 4(b) and (c) display the heating of the battery without and with thermal runaway, respectively, which confirm that the Joule heat did not primarily cause the thermal runaway. In the case of the high-energy laser energy portrayed in Fig. 4(c), the thermal runaway was caused by a combination of the Joule heat and the excess heat energy imparted by laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of laser-irradiated batteries by varying input energy
ce:section.ce:para: Figure A.3(b) illustrates the outer can shape of the battery after the 0.08 kJ laser irradiation of 0.4 s. Fig. A.3(c) depicts a roll taken out from the battery in Fig. A.3(b), and Fig. A.3(d) shows the roll disassembled into four sheets. The upper two sheets were the separators, and the following sheets were the anode and cathode, respectively. The separator and anode sheets had four holes at identical intervals. Evidently, the laser irradiation damaged the outer can (Fig. A.3(b)) as well as the materials inside the battery (Fig. A.3(d)).

Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the X-ray CT image of the battery depicted in Fig. A.3(b), which confirms that damages in the shape of holes were generated in the outer can as well as in the four layers of laminate materials. In addition, a cross-sectional SEM image captured at the damaged part excluding the can is presented in Fig. 5(b). The damage size was 0.4 mm in diameter and 1 mm in depth. The outermost white layer is the Cu sheet of the anode current collector, and the subsequent gray layer is the anode active material. In the following white thick layer, a thin Al sheet of current collector is sandwiched between the cathode active materials.

Fig. 5(c) schematically depicts the image presented in Fig. 5(b). According to the illustration, the separator was removed around the hole, thus generating a small area of contact between the anode and cathode. This caused the soft short circuit observed in Fig. 4(a), which was represented by an OCV drop of 30 mV. Moreover, the variation in OCV (Fig. 4(a)) was verified to have caused by the soft short circuit depicted in Fig. 5(b), which was captured under the same laser irradiation conditions. In Fig. 5b), the gray part of a molten trace at the center of the hole was presumed to be the electrolyte, as F was detected by an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy.

Figs. A.5(a)–(d) illustrates the images of the battery disassembled after laser irradiation of 4.6 kJ (irradiation time of 80 s) earlier depicted in Fig. 4(b). Figure A.5(a) portrays the initiation of the unfolding of the roll inside the battery. The center black portion, indicated by the arrow, is the laser-irradiated section. In addition, Fig. A.5(b) depicts the completely unrolled battery in which the laser-irradiated section is represented by eleven holes. Similarly, Fig. A.5(c) displays the sheet in Fig. A.5(b) disassembled into two sheets, wherein the upper and lower sheets are the cathode active material and Cu current collector, respectively. Moreover, Fig. A.5(d) exhibits a magnified view of Fig. A.5(c). On the upper sheet, the gray circles observed around the holes represent the exposed Al current collector. Furthermore, the separator sheet was not observed in Figs. A.5(a)–(d), thus facilitating the prediction that the sheet was either carbonized or burned.

Fig. 6 presents a cross-sectional SEM micrograph of the other battery with a SOC of 0% after the laser irradiation of 4.6 kJ, similar to the case presented in Fig. 4(b). This laser irradiation was performed to clarify the influence of the 4.6 kJ energy irradiated to the battery material free from Joule heat. In Fig. 6, eight layers of laminate materials were found to be broken, which generated a hole resembling the keyhole welding in laser processing [10–13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal phenomena of laser irradiated batteries by varying input energy
ce:section.ce:para: The maximum temperature of the laser-irradiated batteries observed in the above experiments are summarized in Table 2 , which were extracted from the results presented in Fig. 4 (SOC 100%). Only in case 1, the temperature was measured on the battery presented in Fig. 6. The schematic illustrations in Table 2 depict the locations of laser irradiations on the batteries and the damaged parts of the separators as orange and blue markers, respectively. The damaged parts in cases 1 and 2 were only around the laser irradiated parts, whereas all the separators in the batteries disappeared in cases 3 and 4.

Moreover, the SOC values in cases 1 and 3 varied significantly. Subsequently, we compared cases 2 and 3 of the soft and hard short circuits, respectively, which were previously explained in Fig. 4(a) and (b), respectively. Thus, the variation between soft and hard short circuits originated from the difference in the lost area of the separator. Ultimately, comparing cases 3 and 4, the maximum temperature attained in case 4 was higher than that in case 3. The higher temperature accelerated the thermal runaway expressed in Fig. 4(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal phenomena of batteries heated by laser, ceramic heater, and ARC furnace
ce:section.ce:para: A ceramic heater test was conducted. The temporal variations of the battery surface temperature and OCV when the battery was heated by a 40-W ceramic heater are illustrated in Fig. 7 . The heating rate of the heater was approximately 20 °C min–1, which was immediately stopped at 10.7 min after the OCV was confirmed to have decreased to 0 V. After stopping the heater, rapid heat generation was observed and thermal runaway occurred subsequently.

Fig. 8 (a) presents a comparison between the temperature variations of dT/dt for laser irradiation, ceramic heater, and ARC. The data in the plots have been extracted from Fig. 3(c) (ARC), 4(c) (laser), and 7 (heater). The arrows in Fig. 8(a) indicate the instant at which the laser irradiation/heating was stopped. For the ceramic heater and ARC, an increase in the dT/dt was observed at approximately 175 °C, which corresponds to the first deformation temperature of the separator in Fig. 1(c), suggesting the generation of Joule heat based on the hard short circuit. In Fig. 8(a), the increase in dT/dt under laser irradiation starts at a temperature well below 175 °C, which can be considered to result from the existing temperature gradient, because the laser-irradiated area was notably smaller than the ceramic-heated area. Based on this consideration, the dT/dt profiles of the laser-irradiated battery and the ceramic heater were similar—in the temperature range of 150–300 °C.

Fig. 8(b) displays the OCV data corresponding to Fig. 8(a), wherein the arrows in the figure indicate the points where the OCVs decrease to 0 V. In the cases of laser irradiation and the ceramic heater, both OCVs attained 0 V at 175 °C. Therefore, the OCV profiles of these heating techniques were similar.

Based on the above discussion, the thermal runaway process by laser irradiation can be compared to that of the ARC as follows: the Joule heat generated by the hard short circuit and additional input energy elevated the temperature of the battery above 220 °C, which caused further heat generation by decomposition reaction of the battery materials, thus resulting in thermal runaway. Moreover, the dT/dt behavior of the ceramic heater and laser irradiation presented in Fig. 8(a) were similar. Therefore, the laser irradiation can produce the same kind of heating as the ceramic heater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of the laser irradiation procedure
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions of thermal runaway in response to the three heating methods used in this study: laser irradiation, ceramic heater, and ARC furnace are summarized in Table 3 , wherein the laser-heated areas of the battery, and those heated by the ceramic heater and the ARC furnace are schematically represented using red markers. In addition, the heating area, input energy, power per unit area, heating time, and heating type are listed in the table as well. The presented data evidently show that the laser irradiation involved the smallest heating area, the lowest input energy, and the shortest heating time.

As compared to the ceramic heater, the laser irradiation caused thermal runaway with a lower input energy and a shorter heating time, which is attributed to the high input power per limited area; thus, the heating occurred locally. As discussed above, heating by both laser irradiation and ceramic heater caused the same magnitudes of dT/dt at temperatures above 150 °C, although their input energies per unit area varied.
Based on the features of a high-power local heating, the practical uses of laser irradiation can be effective in the following cases.
•In tests that cause thermal runaway in a target battery without increasing the temperature of adjacent batteries in a module. This can be used for assessing propagation performance.

•In thermal runaway tests with a local internal short circuit inside a battery with an additional local heat generation. Laser irradiation can simulate an internal short circuit by the formation of a molten trace, which varies from laser impact [9].
•In thermal runaway tests of batteries with a small amount of input energy for a short time duration.

The thermal runaway process of a battery generated by laser irradiation was studied, and the characteristics of laser irradiation performance were determined by comparing them to those of an ARC and a ceramic heater.
1)
The thermal runaway process caused by the laser irradiation proceeded as follows: the separator shrank when the irradiated part of the battery material was heated, which resulted in an internal short circuit. Subsequently, an exothermic reaction occurred. However, thermal runaway did not occur owing to this process alone, as thermal runaway requires a temperature above 220 °C that necessitates additional heating.
2)
The deformation and disappearance of the separator resulted in soft and hard short circuits.
3)
As compared to the ceramic heater, laser irradiation can cause thermal runaway with a lower input energy and shorter heating period.

In conclusion, the laser irradiation to the battery was effective in generating thermal runaway in a highly controlled manner. Specifically, this technique is considered to be an efficient initiation method in thermal propagation tests that do not increase the temperature of an adjacent battery in a module, and it constitutes as one of the practical uses of laser irradiation. Our findings are expected to be extremely useful for thermal propagation tests of other types and sizes of battery materials.

Sayoko Shironita: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curtion. Hideki Tsuruga: Investigation, Data curtion. Keizoh Honda: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Kenichiroh Koshika: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Minoru Umeda: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been partially supported by the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT), Japan. The authors acknowledge Dr. T. Honma and Ms. Y. Du for their assistance with the TMA and DSC measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.est.2021.102715.
